heading: PROEMIO 5
preview: This chunk constitutes the programmatic preface to the body of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi makes two interlocking claims: (1) divine truth is inaccessible to unaided reason and requires purification of the soul as its precondition; and (2) the path upward from sensible creation to the invisible God runs through harmonic analogy, with Number as the universal medium connecting all levels of being. Together these claims justify both the mystical orientation and the encyclopaedic disciplinary scope of the whole treatise. The passage opens with a sharp critique of philosophical rationalism. Those who rely on "argomentazioni intricate" drawn from sensory evidence mistake the domain of natural philosophy for the domain of the divine. Divine realities "si mostrano soltanto grazie alla luce divina" and "respingono i superbi e i sapienti del mondo, mentre accolgono e volentieri nutrono i piccoli e gli umili di cuore." The Pauline echo (cf. 1 Cor.

heading: PROEMIO 9
preview:
The opening of this chunk completes Zorzi's methodological hierarchy of disciplines in the *Proemio*. Different orders of reality require different instruments: astronomical harmony is resolved by musical calculation; the supreme intelligences and arcana beyond the heavens require the prophets and the saints, or the invocation of the Light itself. Yet there is a single path that lifts the mind from visible things up to the penetralia of heaven: the path of number. > «percorreremo (abbandonata la peregrinazione dell'errore) un'unica via: il cammino dei numeri, mediante i quali queste realtà inferiori si mostrano a chi le contempla nella loro connessione con i mondi superiori, in virtù della loro dolcissima proporzione armonica e della reciproca corrispondenza.»
p. 177
Number is not merely a tool of calculation but an ontological mediator: it "ascends easily to heaven" by reason of its kinship with the superior worlds, while also having "great familiarity with sensible realities." This double nature — anchored below, akin to what is above — makes number the ideal ladder of contemplation. Zorzi cites Proclus (footnote 13 in Campanini's edition) for the claim that number, though essentially always the same, manifests differently according to the level of reality in which it is instantiated: it is one thing in the voice, another in the proportions of things, another in the soul and reason, another still in the divine worlds. The same number that is determined in concrete realities is found, stripped of all determination, in the proximity of God.

heading: PROEMIO 13
preview:
Chunk 41 (pp. 181–185) sits at the climax of the long Proem and performs two intertwined tasks: (1) it consolidates the visionary warrant for the work — reading Ezekiel's chariot, Moses at Sinai, and the Johannine Prologue as converging testimonies to a hidden cosmic harmony — and (2) it announces the architectonic plan of the three *cantica* that will organise the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*. The theological key throughout is the Kabbalistic-Neoplatonic identification of the *Verbum* / Logos with the intelligible world that contains and sustains the sensible. The chunk opens mid-sentence in Ezekiel's visionary description of the *Merkavah* (chariot throne). Zorzi quotes the Hebrew:
> *haophan betoc haophan*, cioè una ruota in mezzo a un'altra ruota
p. 181
This phrase — Ezekiel 1:16, the *ophanim* or "wheel within a wheel" — is read cosmologically: the mundane world of rotary, spherical form is situated *in the midst of* the intelligible sphere, which is itself identified with the *Verbum*. The Johannine gloss ("ciò che è stato fatto, in lui era la vita") is cited immediately (John 1:3–4): creation exists *in* the Word, which is the centre of the supramundane circle.

heading: PROEMIO 17
preview:
Chunk 42 spans two distinct but continuous textual moments: the closing paragraphs of Zorzi's dedicatory Proem to Pope Clement VII (concluding the long preamble begun earlier) and the opening of the first chapter of Caput I, which asks "Who is the progenitor of the world?" Together they establish both the formal architecture of the *De Harmonia Mundi* and its epistemological ground — the claim that genuine knowledge of God requires divine illumination and flows through a continuous chain of enlightened figures from Adam onward. ---
Zorzi announces that the entire work will be organized into **three Cantici**, each comprising eight tones, forming a perfect octave. The structural logic mirrors the cosmological argument: the Creator himself organized creation through the octave, resting on the seventh day and completing the pattern in the eighth, which is simultaneously identical and non-identical with the first ("che coincide e nel contempo non coincide con il primo"). This recursive identity is the formal signature of harmony. > Abbiamo creduto di poter abbracciare interamente questo accordo in tre cantici modulati sull'ottava, ciascun cantico sarà composto di otto toni, formando perciò un'ottava perfetta. Nel primo cantico faremo risuonare la consonanza reciproca tra entrambi i mondi e tra essi e l'Archetipo. p.

heading: SUPERECCELSA
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of the "Supereccelsa" section (approximately the first 300 lines of the file, corresponding to the first two chapters of the Cantico Primo). The full section runs to p. 332 and contains substantially more material not captured here.*
Zorzi opens the "Supereccelsa" with a programmatic epistemological declaration: genuine knowledge of God is impossible without the illuminating divine light (*lumen divinum*). He grounds this immediately in the Platonic *Timaeus* — the difficulty of finding and speaking worthily about "the author and parent of the universe" — and in the Psalmist's verse *In tua luce videbimus lumen* (Ps. 36:9). Without this light, all philosophical discourse about God goes astray. > «Trovare l'autore e il genitore dell'universo è altrettanto difficile quanto, dopo averlo trovato, parlarne in modo degno»: dimora infatti in una luce inaccessibile.

heading: TONUS SECUNDUS
preview:
The *Tonus Secundus* opens with an extended application of Zorzi's governing musical metaphor to the domain of pagan and secular philosophy. The chapter title — "Quanta dissonantia absoni reperiantur mundi sapientes in suis dogmatibus" — announces the inversion of the harmony theme that organizes *De Harmonia Mundi* as a whole. Here, harmony is defined by its absence: the "mundi sapientes," the wise men of this world, produce not concord but catastrophic dissonance. Zorzi opens with a definition of harmony drawn explicitly from musical theory:
> *harmonia, quae (ut musici diffiniunt) est concentus nervorum, aut vocum in integros modos sine aliqua offensione consonantium*
p. 332
This definition — harmony as the consonance of strings or voices in complete modes without any offense — is offered as the standard the pagan philosophers manifestly fail to meet. The phrase "sine aliqua offensione" is significant: it implies that dissonance is not merely aesthetic failure but a kind of moral and cognitive offense, a wounding of right order. The bulk of the chapter is a pointed doxographical catalogue of irreconcilable disagreements within and between the major philosophical schools.

heading: E QUANTI LI HANNO DESCRITTI
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (approx. the first 6000 words / through chapter 5 of the section). Later sub-sections on pp. 350–365 are not represented in detail.*
---
The overarching argument of this chapter is musical and metaphysical at once. Zorzi opens with a definition drawn from music theory — harmony is the coordination of strings and voices according to rules of consonance without violation — and deploys it as a cosmological criterion. The divergence of secular philosophers is not merely intellectual error but an acoustic catastrophe: they produce a *strepitus horribilis* that cannot be resolved into consonance. The reason, Zorzi argues by invoking Boethius, is ontological: *omne enim ideo est quia unum numero est* — everything exists insofar as it is one.

heading: Libro del decennio o della decina, il Tesoro delle vite, il Segreto della
preview:
This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 46 (pp. 365–410), a very long section whose full heading lists several kabbalistic titles: *Libro del decennio o della decina*, *il Tesoro delle vite*, *il Segreto della fede*, *il Lume per gli occhi*, *la Corona del buon nome*, *il Sefer Temunot*, *la Mercavah di Ezechiele*, *il trattato Sui segreti delle preghiere*, and *Imre shefer* (Discorsi di bellezza). This opening enumeration places Zorzi's project in explicit continuity with the canon of medieval Kabbalah. Zorzi begins by rehearsing the transmission lineage (*Tradizione*) of esoteric doctrine from Moses through the Sanhedrin's seventy elders, stressing that admission to this college required mastery of languages and arcane truths. He traces the succession through Simeon the Just, Antigonus, Yosef ben Yoezer and Yosef ben Yohanan, Yehoshua ben Perahiyah, Hillel and Shammai, and the Tannaitic sages through Yohanan ben Zakkai and his circle — the lineage that divided into the Talmudic and Kabbalistic branches. Paul, schooled under Gamaliel in this very Sanhedrin, and John stand out among the apostles as the ones who penetrated the deepest layer of these mysteries. Among commentators, Pseudo-Dionysius and Origen are singled out as the most penetrating, though Origen — bound by his oath to Ammonius not to divulge his master's teachings — always "si mantenne sempre sulla superficie, ma in maniera tale da alludere, a beneficio degli iniziati, al midollo più riposto."
Zorzi identifies Ezra as the first to commit the oral tradition to writing — in seventy volumes corresponding to the number of elders — on divine orders to preserve it against the disruption of the Babylonian exile.

heading: TONUS TERTIUS
preview:
The opening chapter of the Tonus Tertius poses the cosmological question of how the One (monas) diffuses itself into multiplicity without losing its essential unity. Zorzi's answer is harmonic proportion: the monad extends itself through the ratios of Platonic musical harmony, arriving at the number twenty-seven — the cube of three — as the structural limit of this diffusion. Zorzi grounds the discussion explicitly in the *Timaeus*, noting that Plato followed Timaeus Locrus in constructing the World Soul through a series of doublings and triplings from the monad:
> Plato Timaeum Locrum imitatus animae, et mundi fabricam describit per acceptam monadem, et per duplatas, triplatas, et quadriplatas proportiones usque ad viginti et septem, cubum videlicet primi numeri devenit, addens omnia intervalla repleta fuisse. p. 410
The series 1–2–3–4–9–8–27 (the Platonic lambda) yields 27 as the cube of 3, identified here as "cubum primi numeri" — the cube of the first (odd) number. This is the structural scaffolding of both the World Soul and the cosmos as a whole. Zorzi then turns to the major ancient commentators on the *Timaeus* — Proclus and Chalcidius, along with later interpreters ("posteri") — who make a technical substitution: instead of beginning the harmonic series from 1 (the monad itself), they place six (the first perfect number) in the position of unity:
> senarium primum perfectum numerum unitatis loco collocant, quem eodem ordine duplando, triplandoque usque ad 162 deveniunt.

heading: VERSO TUTTE LE CREATURE
preview:
Pages 411–421 constitute the opening of what Zorzi calls the exposition of the *concordia trium Enneadum* — the harmonic concord of the three novenaries that structure created reality. The two chapters present a dense synthesis of Platonic arithmetic, Kabbalistic exegesis of Genesis, Pythagorean cosmology, Hermetic trinitarian theology, and Pauline-Aristotelian natural philosophy. The driving thesis is that the One necessarily unfolds into multiplicity through the ternary principle, and that this unfolding carries an intrinsic harmonic structure all the way from the Archetype to the lowest material things. ---
Zorzi opens with the problem inherited from Plato's *Timaeus* (and its Pythagorean source Timaeus of Locri): how can the monad's unity cohere with multiplicity without fracture? The answer is harmonic proportion. Following Plato, Zorzi notes that the World-Soul was constructed from the monad through double and triple ratios until the number twenty-seven (3³, the cube of the first number) was reached. Proclus and Calcidius, he notes, substitute the number six (the first perfect number) for the unit, reaching 162 by the same doubling-tripling procedure, in order to fill all the musical intervals — fifths, octaves, tones, semitones, and lemmas — so that the entire Soul and World are perfectly harmonized.

heading: libro dell'Ecclesiastico: Io sola ho percorso l'intera volta celeste e sono
preview: This chunk opens the Tono Terzo of the Cantico Primo and is one of the most cosmologically comprehensive sections of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi's governing argument is that the entire created order — angelic, celestial, and terrestrial — unfolds according to the ternary principle derived from the divine Trinity itself. The triad of enneads (three nines) structures reality at every level, with each nine-fold series completed and transcended by a tenth element of a qualitatively different nature. The summary below covers the opening portion of this section (approximately pp. 421–470), as the chunk runs to eighty-plus pages. Zorzi opens by citing the Ecclesiasticus/Sirach passage on Wisdom's circuit of the heavens as the scriptural anchor for his cosmology. The relationship between centre (Spirito) and circumference (the vault of heaven) designates the Spirit and the bond of mutual love.

heading: TONUS QUARTUS
preview:
The fiftieth chunk of *De Harmonia Mundi* opens the **Tonus Quartus** (Fourth Tone) with its programmatic first chapter: *Quomodo angeli primi ordinis conveniant cum primo coelo, quod primum mobile dicitur* ("How the angels of the first order correspond to the first heaven, which is called the primum mobile"). This title announces the governing method of the entire Fourth Tone: to map each angelic order onto its corresponding celestial sphere, establishing a harmonic consonance between the intelligible and the material heavens. The chapter is brief — barely a page — but it sets the cosmological and exegetical scaffolding for everything that follows. Zorzi opens by marking a deliberate methodological shift:
> *Postquam de trium enneadum genitura disseruimus, ad ipsarum concordiam, unius videlicet fabricae cum alia particularem consonantiam deveniamus.*
p. 504
Having completed the section on the "generation" (*genitura*) of the three enneads — the triple nine-fold structure of angels, heavens, and sublunary things — Zorzi now turns to their *concordia*: the particular consonance (*particularem consonantiam*) of one fabric with another. This is the heart of the *harmonia mundi* thesis: the universe is not merely layered but harmonically interlocked, and the task of the philosopher-theologian is to chart those interlockings precisely. Zorzi's primary authority for the angelic hierarchy is Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, whom he calls "our Areopagite Dionysius, true speculator of arcana" (*Ariopagitam nostrum Dionysium verum arcanorum speculatorem*).

heading: L'uno l'altro e in rapporto all'Archetipo
preview:
This summary covers the opening portion of Chunk 51, which is an exceptionally long section (61,586 words). The essay draws from approximately the first 600 lines of the text, corresponding to the first three numbered chapters of Cantico Primo, Tono Quarto. The full section, running from pages 505 to 676, continues far beyond what is surveyed here and likely extends the same principle of angelic-celestial concordance through all nine levels of both hierarchies. The central project of this section is announced in its title: **"L'uno l'altro e in rapporto all'Archetipo"** ("Each [created order] in relation to the other and to the Archetype"). After completing his account of the three enneads (angelic, celestial, and sublunary), Zorzi now undertakes a systematic demonstration that every member of the angelic hierarchy corresponds by analogy to a specific celestial sphere, and that both together mirror the divine Archetype. This is the great harmonic thesis of the *De Harmonia Mundi* applied at the level of fine structural detail. ---
Zorzi opens by invoking **Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite** as the foundational authority, presented as the authentic investigator of the deepest arcana, whose teaching derived from Paul or from Hierotheus.

heading: TONUS QUINTUS — Caput I: Cur vigesimo septimo numero a Platone fiat illa prima descriptio
preview:
The Fifth Tone (*Tonus Quintus*) opens with a tightly focused question in its very first chapter: why does Plato's primordial description of the world-soul culminate precisely in the number twenty-seven? The chapter is brief — barely a page — but it serves as a technical preamble for what will follow, grounding Zorzi's Kabbalistic and Christian harmonic cosmology in the most authoritative strands of ancient mathematical philosophy. The question is not merely arithmological curiosity; for Zorzi, the answer illuminates the deep structure of creation. Zorzi opens by invoking the consensus of "all Pythagoreans and Academics" (*Omnibus pythagoricis, et academicis manifestissimum est*) that the world and the soul were described first by Timaeus Locrus and afterward by Plato according to certain musical laws and proportions. The construction proceeds from the number one, doubled to the cube of two (1, 2, 4, 8) and tripled to the cube of three (1, 3, 9, 27), yielding seven limit-numbers that compose a kind of heptachord:
> *mundum, et animam primo a Timaeo Locro, postea a Platone describi quibusdam legibus, et proportionibus musicis, veluti heptachordum quoddam ex septem limitibus confectum, incipiendo ab unitate, duplando usque ad cubum binarii, triplando usque ad cubum, et solidum ternarii*
p. 676
This is the classic Platonic lambda (Λ), the seven numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, 27 that ancient commentators read into the *Timaeus* (35a–36b) as encoding the harmonic ratios by which the Demiurge ordered the soul and cosmos. Zorzi follows the tradition without deviation here, treating it as an established metaphysical fact that these proportions "dispose and complete the fabric of the soul and the entire world, according to the monuments of Pythagoras" (*iuxta Pythagorae monumenta*).

heading: GLI INTERVALLI DEI GENERI PRIMARI
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of the chapter (approximately the first 6,000 words / roughly chapters I–X of the section). The full section extends to p. 728 and likely continues the macrocosm-microcosm argument and further Kabbalistic applications; those later passages are not reflected here.*
---
Zorzi opens by affirming what "all Pythagoreans and Academics" know: that both the world and the soul were described first by **Timaeus of Locri** and then by **Plato** (in the *Timaeus*) according to musical proportions, using a series beginning with unity, doubling to the cube of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8) and tripling to the cube of 3 (1, 3, 9, 27). These are the seven intervals of an eight-stringed instrument. The even numbers express the "feminine" principle, the odd numbers the "masculine," and from their combination all things are generated. The upper limit of the entire structure is the cube of each series, because spatial reality cannot go beyond three dimensions (length, breadth, depth). Zorzi surveys the graphical representations of this series: **Adrastus** and **Calcidius** arrange the numbers in a triangle with unity at the apex; **Porphyry**, **Severus**, and **Proclus** prefer three columns; Proclus, following Plato most closely, collapses all seven terms onto a single line: 1.

heading: libro de formatione, ab eo (ut asseritur) compilato, ubi ex connotatis
preview:
Chunk 54 spans three chapters (XVI–XVIII) of the First Canticle, Fifth Tone, pages 728–750 of Campanini's Bompiani edition. The section opens with a transitional paragraph on Abraham and the *Sefer Yetzirah* as the highest vehicle of cosmological knowledge, moves through an elaborate numerical cosmology built on the triad number/measure/weight, surveys the history of musical string-theory as a microcosm of cosmic harmony, and culminates in a dense Kabbalistic analysis of the divine names *ehyeh asher ehyeh* and the Tetragrammaton. The central thesis is that the entire created order, from the nine angelic choirs through the nine heavens to the nine orders of sublunary things, is the cubic unfolding of the divine Ternary, and that God inscribed this structure into the letters and numerical values of His own supreme Name. ---
The chunk opens in mid-sentence, recapping the *Libro della formazione* (*Sefer Yetzirah*), said to have been compiled by Abraham himself. Zorzi explains that the book uses the letters of Hebrew names, combined with their rotations (galgal), permutations, numerical equivalents (gematria), vowel points, and cantillation accents to point toward the quiddities of things:
> «sulla base delle caratteristiche delle lettere che formano i nomi in ebraico, per mezzo delle loro rotazioni e permutazioni, delle cifre equivalenti alle lettere, nonché dei punti vocalici e degli accenti, si ha un sistema di allusioni alle quiddità delle cose e alle loro corrispondenze, una tecnica degna di meraviglia, più adatta alla contemplazione che alla spiegazione particolareggiata.»
p. 728–729
This passage situates Kabbalistic letter-mysticism as a contemplative rather than merely discursive art. It follows a rich epistemological preamble (just before this chunk begins) in which Zorzi contrasts Platonic anamnesis, Aristotelian tabula rasa, and the "death of a kiss" (*mors osculi*) taught by the most secret theologians — citing 1 John 2:20 and 2:27, John 14:26, Psalms 116:15, and 2 Corinthians 3:18.

heading: TONUS SEXTUS — De modo procedendi, et concordia generali hominis cum mundo maiori, et Archetypo. Caput I
preview:
The opening chapter of the *Tonus Sextus* announces its subject as *"concordia generali hominis cum mundo maiori, et Archetypo"* — the general concordance of man with the greater world and with the Archetype. Before engaging that theme directly, Zorzi pauses to reflect on the *modus procedendi* — the proper mode of proceeding — a methodological prologue that is characteristic of his self-conscious literary practice. Zorzi opens with an appeal to the visual arts and to a Pythagorean authority:
> Pictores naturae aemuli accuratissimi ea, quae figurant, quoad possunt, sedulo imitantur. Nam pictura (inquit Apollonius pythagoreus) est imitatio quaedam rei naturalis, a natura comparata, sed colorum compositio est ab arte. In prima autem potior pingendi vis collocata est. p. 750
The citation is attributed to *Apollonius pythagoreus* — Apollonius of Tyana, the Neopythagorean philosopher and wonder-worker whose *Vita* by Philostratus was well known in Renaissance humanist circles.

heading: COME IN UN MICROCOSMO
preview: This chunk inaugurates the extended treatment of man as microcosm (the sixth *Tono* of the *Cantico Primo*). Zorzi's governing thesis is that the human being stands as the middle term and closing bracket of a cosmic circle: the Archetypal idea in the divine Word is one extreme, all created things form the middle, and man is the other extreme — most recently produced yet most similar to the Creator. Because "the work must imitate the Artificer," and because God (per Hermes) is a sphere and circle, man must also be comprehended within a spherical figure. The three worlds (Archetype, macrocosm, man) are enclosed in a single circle. Zorzi opens with a reflection on mimesis and writing craft, comparing painters who imitate nature to historians, poets, and orators. Moses is held up as the supreme model: he narrated the creation of the macrocosm with a simple command, but reserved a far more elaborate and solemn narration for the creation of the microcosm, repeating it twice (man "created," then "made," then "breathed into"). This double or triple register in Genesis is read as intentional mystery-writing befitting a creation of incomparably greater dignity.

heading: libro del trattato dedicato ad Almansore38, e anche Galeno39, Avicenna40, Alberto Magno41, Michele Scoto42, il Conciliatore43 e molti nostri
preview:
**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (approx. the first 600–800 lines out of ~3,600), supplemented by chapter headings and spot-checks of later chapters. The full section runs from Caput V to Caput XXXIV. ---
The chunk opens mid-argument within a discussion of physiognomy and the reading of character from physical signs — attributed to Pythagoras, to Fraotes king of India (via Apollonius of Tyana), to Julius Caesar and Sulla, and supported by a citation from Job ("Ha inciso sul palmo della mano degli uomini"). Zorzi notes that physicians read bodily constitution from the colour of the face, offering an elaborate Galenic-Avicennian scheme of the four humours and their chromatic signatures. This opening serves as the porch to the main edifice: the systematic demonstration that the whole cosmos is mirrored in man. ---
The central thesis of the entire section is announced: the "sommo Artefice" has constructed man as a recapitulation of all reality.

heading: TONUS SEPTIMUS
preview:
The opening chapter of the *Tonus Septimus* bears the programmatic rubric: "Si provano con ragioni e autorità che tutte le realtà hanno nell'Archetipo qualcosa cui corrispondono" ("It is proven by arguments and authorities that all things have in the Archetype something to which they correspond"). This frames the entire seventh tone as a sustained demonstration of the doctrine of divine archetypes — the thesis that every created thing has its prior, exemplary cause in God. Zorzi opens with what he calls "sapientum dogma probatissimum" — the most approved and well-worn teaching of the wise — that all fabricated things pre-exist in the craftsman:
> Sapientum dogma probatissimum est, nec minus tritissimum, omnia fabricata praeexistere in artifice prudenter, et non fortuito casu fabricante, pari modo domus in architecto, sic quoque sermo praeexistit in oratore, neque aliter foetura in generante, et praegnante, sic machina haec universitatis in summo progenitore Dei, tamquam vita creatrix praeerat, ad cuius metam omnia membra formaret. p. 900
The analogical series is carefully constructed: as the house pre-exists in the architect, as speech pre-exists in the orator, as offspring pre-exist in the parent, so the "machina haec universitatis" — the whole machine of the universe — pre-exists in God as a "vita creatrix" (creative life). This is a classic Neoplatonic and Augustinian exemplarism, framing God as the divine artist whose mind contains the formal causes of all things. The philosophical core of the chapter rests on Augustine's celebrated triad from the *Book of Wisdom* (11:21): number, weight, and measure (*numerus, pondus, mensura*).

heading: CORRISPONDONO ALL'ARCHETIPO
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 59 (chapters I–V in representative detail, with chapter headings noted through chapter XV). The full section runs to approximately 30,800 words across pages 901–979.*
Zorzi opens with the foundational claim that every created reality pre-existed in its Maker, just as a building pre-exists in the architect's mind. The controlling proof-text is Wisdom 11:20 (number, weight, and measure), interpreted through Augustine: the norms by which things are measured cannot themselves be among the measured things, and since nothing exists outside God and creatures, they must reside in God. Zorzi immediately fuses this with Kabbalistic doctrine, attributing the first disclosure of the Archetype's inner *dimensiones* and *annumerationes* to the patriarch Abraham — a clear reference to the *Sefer Yetzirah*:
> «In Dio vi sono numeri senza numero» (citando Agostino), «poiché tutte le cose che sono in Lui, coincidono con l'unico Dio.»
p. 901 (Italian text)
The scriptural catena that follows is extensive: Ecclesiasticus on counting sand, raindrops, and the days of the age; Psalm 147 (God counts and names the stars); the Gospel saying that God numbers the hairs of the head; Ezekiel's measuring reed (six cubits and a palm); the golden reed of Revelation; Daniel to Belshazzar (*Mane Thecel Phares*); Job on the numbered months of human life; Zechariah's measuring of Jerusalem. Paul's letters to the Romans and Ephesians supply the idea that faith, grace, and even the resurrection body are distributed *secundum mensuram*. The Hebrew term *din* (strict justice, the divine judgment-measure) is introduced and glossed:
> «detta dagli ebrei din, che significa giudizio severo e forte, tanto che nessuno può uscirne giustificato»
p.

heading: libro dei Re, che venne Ezechia – quando giunse Rapsace, inviato dal
preview: Chunk 60 of *De Harmonia Mundi* falls within the Primo Cantico, Tono Settimo, and forms part of Zorzi's sustained meditation on water, purification, and the hierarchical structure of divine blessing. The passage moves through several interconnected typological registers — royal history, liturgical practice, sacramental theology, penitential piety, and eschatology — all organised around the symbolic contrast between life-giving pools (*piscinae*) and death-dealing pits (*laci*, Hebrew *bor*). The chunk opens mid-sentence, referring to the episode from the Second Book of Kings when Rabshakeh (*Rapsace* in the Italian), the general of the Assyrian king, came to threaten Hezekiah and the people of Jerusalem. Zorzi reads Hezekiah's response as the paradigm of authentic penitence: by turning to God, Hezekiah obtained the "water of grace and divine blessing," and was delivered when 185,000 Assyrian soldiers were struck down in the camp. The upper basin (*bacino superiore*) in Zorzi's schema represents divine blessing dispensed directly from God; the lower basin (*bacino inferiore*) represents the blessing of the saints, which flows downward from what it has received from above. This two-tier structure of benediction is characteristic of Zorzi's emanationist cosmology, in which higher realities overflow into lower ones in a Neoplatonic cascade. Zorzi then introduces the *vasca per lavare* (pool for washing), identified with the Probatica Piscina of John 5:2 — "Vi è a Gerusalemme una vasca probatica" — where the sheep destined for temple sacrifice were washed.

heading: libro della vita, tuttavia è necessario che siano lavate, proprio in quella vasca, della quale si dice nel salmo: Lavami da tutte le mie colpe; e
preview:
**Note:** This summary is based on the opening portion of this very long chunk (44,362 words). The first c.550 lines of text were read in full; chapter headings throughout the remainder were surveyed to establish the structure. The essay therefore provides exhaustive commentary on chapters XVI–XIX and an overview of the subsequent material. ---
Chunk 61 constitutes a continuous encyclopedic survey in the *Settimo Tono* (Seventh Tone) of the *Cantico Primo*. Zorzi's method is identical across every chapter: he takes a natural or geographical entity — a body of water, a meteorological phenomenon, a plant, an animal, a sacred city, an enemy nation — and submits it to a three-level hermeneutic. First the Hebrew name is parsed etymologically; second, all relevant scriptural loci (Old and New Testament) are assembled; third, the referent is mapped onto the Neoplatonic ladder of the soul's ascent from bodily imprisonment to the infinite divine pleroma. Campanini's dual-text edition places the Latin original and Italian translation on facing pages throughout.

heading: TONUS OCTAVUS
preview:
The opening chapter of the *Tonus Octavus* — "Deus omnia complet flatu, et vita suavissima" — marks a programmatic transition in *De Harmonia Mundi*. Having demonstrated in earlier tones that the cosmic house is arranged with its highest guest in the supreme concord of enneads and filled intervals, Zorzi now proposes to make audible the *melos* — the melody — of that house. This melody results, he declares, from the living and unfailing *flatus* (breath) of the Creator who fills all things, a harmony inexplicable in its nature (*inexplicabili harmonia*). Zorzi's key claim, announced in the title and opening lines, is that God fills all things with breath and the sweetest life (*flatu, et vita suavissima*). This is not merely metaphorical: the *flatus Opificis* is the actual ontological ground of cosmic harmony. The move is characteristic of Zorzi's Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic synthesis, where the divine emanation — analogous to the Kabbalistic concept of the divine breath animating creation — becomes the source of musical order. > *omnia complentis inexplicabili harmonia resultat, de qua Orpheus: [Greek] tu totum coelum canora cithara temperas.*

heading: SECONDO L'ARMONIA DEL DIAPASON
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (21 chapters, approximately 42,000 words). The essay is based on a close reading of the first roughly 500 lines of the chunk, supplemented by the chapter-heading structure surveyed throughout.*
The section opens by announcing that, having demonstrated the harmonic structure of the "worldly dwelling" (the cosmos) and its enneadic organisation, Zorzi now turns to making its *melodia* resound — the melody that derives from the "living and immutable breath of the Artificer." The cosmos is not merely structured harmonically; it is actively played as an instrument by God, whose breath (*spiritus*) fills and animates all things. The twenty-one chapters that follow elaborate this cosmological music across three registers: the harmony of the elements, the harmony of the spheres, and the harmony of the angelic intelligences — all of which converge in the diapason (the octave) as the symbol of divine completeness and return. Zorzi opens by citing the Orphic line (preserved in Greek):
> «Tu accordi tutto il cielo sulla cetra sonora»
p. 1095 / line 11
This Orphic testimony is immediately reinforced by the Pythagorean Dorilao (the world is God's musical instrument), Alexander of Miletus, and Gregory Nazianzen (who praises the world's harmony of contraries). Timaeus of Locri, Apuleio, and Augustine's *De ordine* are adduced to affirm that the universe maintains an "indissoluble symmetry" in which contrary forces — male and female voices, treble and bass — resolve into one song under a divine choirmaster. Zorzi then introduces the three levels of Boethian *musica*: *mundana* (elemental), *spherarum* (planetary), and angelic — the soul must purify its inner "eye" to perceive them.

heading: ANTICUM ECUNDUM
preview:
This chunk spans two distinct but connected movements: (1) a preface-dedication to the Secondo Cantico that poses the theological-musical question of what constitutes genuine novelty under Christ, and (2) the opening chapter of the cantico proper, which undertakes a philosophical-theological proof of Christ's divinity as Messiah. Together they frame Book II's agenda: where Book I traced the harmonia mundi through creation, Book II will trace its renewal through the Incarnation and Redemption. ---
Zorzi opens by citing the Psalms' recurring refrain — *canticum novum* — and immediately frames a paradox: if the royal prophet (David) commands a new song, what is genuinely new? Observing with Ecclesiastes ("Nihil sub sole novum") that the cosmos runs in its ancient rhythms — the same sun, same moon, same seasons, same animal species — Zorzi appears to concede that novelty is impossible at the natural level. > *"Perquirebam, an aliqua nova facta sint novo decantanda cantico, cum non aliud coelum, aut sydera novo splendore micantia videamus, nec alia curricula solem per diem, et lunam in nocte peragere..."*
pp. 1206–1207 (Latin text, f. CLXXXVv)
The resolution comes from two Pauline-Johannine witnesses to the Incarnation.

heading: SUL PIANO IDEALE E IN QUANTO UOMO
preview:
This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 65 (approximately pages 1213–1248 in the Campanini/Bompiani edition), a very long chapter within the second *Cantico* of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The material read extends from the incipit through the extended prophetic catena in subsection 2. Given the length of the original (13,659 words), this essay is based on the first approximately 6,000 words of text. The double title — "Sul piano ideale e in quanto uomo" ("On the ideal plane and as man") — signals Zorzi's dual agenda: to argue for Christ's divinity from purely rational-philosophical grounds ("sul piano ideale") and then to confirm it through the typological reading of Hebrew prophecy ("in quanto uomo," i.e., as a historical man born into the Israelite lineage). This is a fully self-conscious exercise in *concord* theology, uniting Neoplatonist participation metaphysics with Christian Kabbalah and patristic exegesis. ---
Zorzi opens by asserting that the messiahship and divinity of Jesus can be demonstrated not only through Scripture and prophecy but through "stringenti argomentazioni razionali" (rigorous rational arguments). The first argument draws on Procline-Neoplatonist theology, citing the testimony of the "great Theodore" preserved by Proclus: "Tutti pregano, eccetto il Primo" — all things pray (i.e., depend upon and receive from) the First Principle, except the First itself.

heading: libro di quella luce, che è detta verità, sono scritte le regole, alle quali
preview:
The chunk opens mid-argument, completing the account of the divine Word as the eternal "book of truth" in which all moral norms are inscribed. Zorzi cites Augustine's doctrine — familiar from the *De Trinitate* and *De libero arbitrio* — that the immutable eternal ideas function like a seal ring impressing wax without departing from the gold: the just law is transferred into the human heart by a process of impression (*per quasi impressionem*), not migration. This Neoplatonic-Augustinian framework is quickly christologized: Augustine's eternal truth is identified with the Veritas who declares to John's Gospel that the murderer "did not stand in the truth from the beginning." Angels themselves depend on this truth, and were they to depart from it they too would become "children of death."
> «La verità immutabile, come una legge eterna vigente in quella corte superna, è consultata dagli angeli. Essi, infatti, non sono per se stessi fonte di verità, ma partecipano della verità creatrice, verso la quale tendono come alla sorgente della vita»
p. 1289/1122 (Italian translation of Augustine)
Paul's declaration — "I resolved to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ, and him crucified" (1 Cor 2:2) — is glossed as the moment when Christ "fulfilled every truth by opening the way toward the end to which we tend, bringing prophecies and the law to completion." The cry from the Cross, *Consummatum est* (John 19:30), is read as the declaration that the totality of mystery and truth was completed. Proverbs 8 (Wisdom speaking: "By me kings reign, and legislators decree justice") is identified with the incarnate Sophia-Christ. Jeremiah's Baruch 3:37 — "He found every way of knowledge and gave it to Jacob his servant" — is cited as the messianic transmission of all law.

heading: TONUS SECUNDUS — Multiplex, et unica est vita omnium. Caput I
preview:
The opening chapter of *Tonus Secundus* announces its organizing thesis in its very title: *Multiplex, et unica est vita omnium* — "The life of all things is multiple and one." This is a cosmological and metaphysical claim with deep roots in Neoplatonic henology: unity and multiplicity are not opposites but poles of a single ontological structure that pervades all levels of being. Zorzi's rhetorical strategy is to work upward by analogy — from observable natural phenomena (the sun, the human soul) to a conclusion about the cosmos as a whole. Zorzi opens by crediting two teachers: the sun, which God kindled to declare his majesty, and the soul, which God fashioned with such excellence. Together they model how one thing can be simultaneously multiple. > Nisi Sol, quem Deus ad declarandam suam maiestatem accendit, et anima, quam tanta excellentia fabricavit, me docerent, quomodo unum possit esse multiplex aut plura, non auderem equidem dicere vitam esse unicam simul atque multiplicem. p. 1304

heading: TONO SECONDO
preview: The "Tono Secondo" opens with the programmatic declaration that **Christ is the life and support of all things** (Cristo è la vita e il sostegno di tutte le cose), that he gives life to all and attracts all things to himself. The first section (§1: "La vita di tutte le cose è molteplice e unica") develops the philosophical groundwork for this claim: life is at once *one* and *multiple*, a paradox that Zorzi resolves by analogy before building his dossier of authoritative witnesses. Zorzi opens with two governing analogies: the *sun* and the *soul*. The sun, which God kindled to manifest his majesty, is one in itself but multiple in its rays, one in essence but highly versatile in power ("unico in sé, ma molteplice nei raggi, unico in essenza ma assai versatile in potenza"). The soul likewise is single yet operates through many organs — hearing in the ears, seeing in the eyes, smelling in the nostrils, and so on — yet remains always the same, undivided even when one organ is impaired. This Neoplatonic motif — the emanation of the One into multiplicity without loss of unity — prepares the ground for the macrocosmic claim: the world is a single living being (*un unico essere vivente*), and the life that animates it is one, just as the soul that animates the human body is one. > «il sole è unico in sé, ma molteplice nei raggi, unico in essenza ma assai versatile in potenza.

heading: libro intitolato Dio è allo stesso tempo nascosto e manifesto, afferma:
preview:
This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 70 (pp. 1309–1394, approximately the first 300 lines of the file as read). The full section is 31,817 words; only the opening segment has been read per the stated methodology. Further sub-chapters beyond those visible in this sample are not summarised here. ---
Chunk 70 belongs to the second Canticle (*Cantico Secondo*, *Tono Secondo*) of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Its overarching concern is the pervasiveness of God in creation — simultaneously hidden and manifest — traced through Hermetic, Orphic, Platonic, Scriptural, and Kabbalistic sources. The section then develops this into a theology of life: all natural and spiritual life flows from a single divine fountain, identified as the Trinitarian God, and Christ is expounded as the Tree of Life at the centre of the Archetype.

heading: TONUS TERTIUS
preview:
The Tonus Tertius opens with a programmatic declaration of the immensity of its subject matter. Zorzi announces the entrance into a "latissimum campum" — a vast field that exceeds the grasp of human vision — as he undertakes a treatment of the priest, understood above all as Christ, the supreme mediator between God and humanity:
> "Latissimum campum, et qui sese ab oculis nostris sua magnitudine tollit, intramus, dum tractare volumus de sacerdote, qui medium inter Deum et hominem tenet, et est quasi Deus visibilis, et maxime summus, et verus sacerdos Christus, cuius sacerdotium pro virili nostra explicare intendimus."
p. 1394
The phrase "quasi Deus visibilis" is charged with Neoplatonic resonance: the priest is the visible image of God among humanity, a mediatorial figure who participates in both the divine and human orders. This positions the priesthood within Zorzi's broader cosmological project of harmonizing levels of being — the priest is the hinge-point, the middle term, in the Neoplatonic triad of divine, intermediate, and material. Zorzi offers a Latin etymology for "sacerdos" that functions as a theological definition: the priest is "quasi sacra dans" — one who gives sacred things. This is not mere wordplay but a structural claim: the priest's identity is constituted by his role as conduit of the sacred, receiving from the "sacrarium supramundanum" (the supramundane sanctuary) and administering to the people. The language of "sacrarium supramundanum" places this at the level of the celestial or intelligible world, consistent with Zorzi's Neoplatonic cosmology in which the divine emanates downward through hierarchical levels.

heading: Cantico la sposa purissima è paragonata dallo sposo a una colomba:
preview:
This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 73 (pp. 1517–1550), a very long section (12,388 words). The text belongs to the *Secondo Cantico* (Tono Terzo) of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* and takes as its point of departure the Song of Songs verse in which the Bridegroom compares his Bride to a dove (*Amica mia, mia colomba, mia bella*). From this lyrical opening Zorzi launches a dense typological and allegorical exegesis of Levitical sacrifice, consistently harmonising Mosaic law, Christology, Neoplatonic faculty-psychology, and Renaissance Christian Kabbalah. ---
The section opens with the pneumatological significance of the dove. Zorzi marshals the Gospel accounts of Christ's baptism (Matthew 3, John 1, Luke 3) to establish that the Holy Spirit chose no other animal for its epiphany. He refines the standard reading: the descent "like a dove" refers not necessarily to the Spirit taking a dove's bodily form, but to the manner of flight — swift, direct, sure — or to some intrinsic purity shared between the bird and the Third Person of the Trinity.

heading: TONUS QUARTUS — Quid sit veritas. Caput I
preview:
Zorzi opens *Tonus Quartus* with a striking epistemological claim: truth is so radiant that it overwhelms weak eyes and exceeds the capacity of those of limited intellectual virtue. Common souls (*vulgarium animi*), not yet purified, take more pleasure in coarse falsehood than in subtle truth. This sets the tone for a chapter that moves from epistemology into Kabbalistic linguistics. > Tantus est veritatis splendor, ut omnes debiles oculos opprimat, et parvam potentiae virtutem excedens offendat, hinc vulgarium animi, et non adhuc bene expiati magis crasso mendacio, quam subtili veritate oblectantur. p. 1550
The definition of truth Zorzi invokes — *rectitudo sola mente perceptibilis* — is the classical Anselmian formula from *De Veritate*, here attributed to "sapientes" by common consent. Truth is perceivable only by the intellect (*mens*), and thus unavailable to those who do not possess mind in the full philosophical sense — those who do not "sapiunt ea quae Dei sunt," who have no taste for divine things.

heading: CHE INSEGNA A TUTTI E ILLUMINA TUTTE LE COSE
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this long section (approximately the first 500 lines of the file, corresponding to chapters 1–4 of the section, pages roughly 1384–1401 of the Campanini edition). The full section extends considerably further.*
---
Zorzi opens with a philosophical definition of truth as "la rettitudine che si percepisce con la sola mente" — a rectitude perceptible by the mind alone — that derives from the "concordia e corrispondenza del termine medio con il principio e la fine." He then grounds this philosophical definition in Hebrew philology. The Hebrew word for truth, **emet** (אמת), is composed of three letters: Alef (א), the first letter of the alphabet; Mem (מ), the middle letter; and Taw (ת), the last letter. This tripartite structure encodes the structure of reality itself: emanation from the beginning, traversal through the middle, and return to the end. > "In essa convergono unite insieme la prima lettera dell'alfabeto, la media e l'ultima. Infatti א, alef, è la prima, ת, taw, l'ultima e מ, mem, quella di mezzo: le tre insieme, vocalizzate con i punti opportuni, si pronunciano emet."
p. 1384
Zorzi extends this into gematria (numerical letter-value analysis): Alef = 1 and 1000 (the cube of ten, the complete number); Mem = 40 (ten multiplied by four, "il numero radicale e il solido fondamento di tutti i numeri"); Taw = 400 (ten times forty, or four times one hundred).

heading: TONUS QUINTUS — Quomodo Deus sit pater omnium. Caput I
preview:
The seventy-sixth chunk inaugurates the *Tonus Quintus* of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* with a programmatic chapter headed *Quomodo Deus sit pater omnium* ("How God is the father of all," Caput I, pp. 1644–1645). The chapter's declared goal is to explain in what sense first the eternal Father and then Christ can properly be called *pater omnium*. Rather than opening with scripture or Kabbalistic authority, Zorzi deploys a characteristically syncretic move: he grounds the theological claim in natural philosophy, reading corporeal generation as a *simulacrum* and *imago* of divine generation. The argument rests on a principle he states explicitly — that in inferior things there are shadows and images of divine realities (*in his inferioribus sint simulachra, et imagines rerum divinarum*) — a thoroughly Neoplatonic axiom that runs throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*. The vehicle for the analogy is a survey of ancient and medieval medical opinion on the *origo seminis*. Zorzi enumerates four positions:

heading: INFLUISCE SU TUTTE LE MEMBRA
preview:
*Note: This summary covers the opening portion (approximately the first 6,000 words) of a very long section (35,971 words). Later sub-sections are not covered here.*
---
The section opens with a programmatic statement of Zorzi's comparative method: in order to explain how the eternal Father and Christ are called "father of all things," it is necessary first to recover the natural philosophers' accounts of biological generation, because in lower realities there are "simulacra and images of divine realities." The analogical movement — from physiology upward to theology, and from theology back down to spiritual practice — governs the entire opening sequence. Zorzi surveys the ancient debate on the origin of seed: **Hippocrates** locates it in the brain (the foamy, carnal portion); **Aristotle** in the heart as seat of the soul; **Galen** in the liver as seat of digestive and generative power. **Avicenna**, synthesizing all three, argues that seed comes from all three principal organs. A further position — which Zorzi ultimately endorses — holds that seed is secreted from every member of both parents, so that each part of the offspring resembles the corresponding part of the parent:
> *"Et così il cuore è generato dal cuore del generante, l'occhio dall'occhio, la mano dalla mano e così via per ciascun membro, benché la potenza e lo spirito provengano principalmente dal cervello, dal cuore e dal fegato, che sono le membra principali e le più spirituali."*
p. 1478 (Latin) / p. 1479 (Italian translation)
---
The physiological analogy is immediately applied to Christology.

heading: TONUS SEXTUS — Christus est author, et finis fidei (Caput I)
preview:
Chunk 78 opens the *Tonus Sextus*, the sixth major tonal division of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The chapter heading — *Christus est author, et finis fidei* ("Christ is the author and end of faith") — signals a turn from the cosmological and kabbalistic harmonics of the earlier tones toward a more explicitly Christological and theological register. The source pages (1738–1739 of the Campanini edition) present the opening argument of what will be an extended meditation on Christ as the organizing centre of Christian revelation. Zorzi opens by citing Paul's Epistle to the Hebrews as his warrant for the claim that Christ is both the *author* and *consummator* of faith. He is explicit about his epistemic caution: he would not dare to assert this himself, but Paul's authority licenses it:
> Tot videntur credenda, quae non sunt Christus IESUS, quod nullatenus dicere auderem ipsum authorem, et finem fidei, nisi id me doceret Paulus, in cuius pectore ille resonat. p. 1738
The passage from Hebrews that Zorzi quotes reads:
> Occurramus in oppositum nobis certamen suspicientes in authorem fidei, et consummatorem IESUM.

heading: DI TUTTE LE OPERE DANDO A TUTTI
preview:
**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (27,630 words). The analysis is based on the first approximately 500 lines of the text, which correspond to the introductory chapters of the Tono Sesto and its subsections up through Chapter V. The remaining pages, which continue the enumeration of Christ's names and the transition to the theme of "giving to all," are not covered here. ---
The chunk opens the sixth "tone" (Tono Sesto) of the Secondo Cantico under the sub-heading *La facoltà di operare* ("The Faculty of Operating"). Zorzi's first chapter argues that Christ is not merely an object of faith among others but the very author (*author*) and perfecter (*consummator*) of faith and of all creaturely operations. The argument is anchored in Paul's Letter to the Hebrews, which he quotes as the governing lemma:
> «Corriamo con perseveranza nella corsa che ci sta davanti, tenendo fisso lo sguardo su GESÙ, autore e perfezionatore della fede.»
p. 1571 (Italian text, lines 10–11)
Zorzi's move is characteristically synthetic: natural reason can demonstrate that God exists and created the world (he notes that even Aristotle, "awakened from the sleep of the senses," does not dissent from other sages on God as Artificer), but the fact that God created everything *through the incarnate Word* is knowable only from Scripture.

heading: TONUS SEPTIMUS
preview: The opening of *Tonus Septimus* — titled in the Italian heading "Cristo è il re dei re e il supremo imperatore" (Christ is the King of Kings and the Supreme Emperor) — launches a sustained Christological reading of Mosaic Scripture. The chapter heading announces the program: *Christum esse verum Dominum vaticiniis Mosaicis praenuntiatum* — "That Christ is the true Lord foretold by the Mosaic prophecies." This is the first chapter (*Caput I*) of the seventh *Tonus*, and it marks a shift from cosmological harmonics toward prophetic theology and the exegesis of Genesis as messianic prefiguration. Zorzi's central exegetical problem is the double account of humanity's creation in Genesis. The first account (Gen. 1:27) states that God created humanity — male and female — in his image and likeness; the second (Gen. 2:7) speaks as though nothing had been said before, describing the formation of man from the dust of the earth (*de limo terrae*) and the breathing of the breath of life (*spiraculum vitarum*). Similarly, after relating the creation of male and female, the text appears to forget this and adds that no suitable helper was found for the man.

heading: CHE GOVERNA E MODERA TUTTE LE COSE
preview:
Chunk 81 belongs to the *Secondo Cantico*, Tono Settimo, of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* and constitutes one of his most sustained exercises in prophetic Christology read through Kabbalistic philology. The presiding claim — announced in the section title — is that Christ is the true ruler who governs and moderates all things. Zorzi builds this case exegetically, marching through Mosaic, Davidic, Isaian, and Jeremiac testimony, at each step deploying Aramaic targumim and Hebrew philology to foreclose non-Christological Jewish readings. ---
Zorzi opens with a problem in Genesis: Moses appears to narrate the creation of humanity twice. A man of Moses's stature — "eruditissimo in ogni forma di sapienza, ricolmo di Dio e perfuso dallo Spirito santo" — could not simply have forgotten what he wrote. The solution, drawn from "sacrosanct receptacles" (*sacrosancti receptacula*), the standard Zorzian term for kabbalistic sources (likely referring to the Zohar or to the Sefirot-based commentary tradition), is that the first creation account describes the Messiah and his mother:
> Di quei due Cristo è la vera immagine di Dio e il primogenito di tutte le cose, come dice Paolo: Egli è l'immagine del Dio invisibile, il primogenito di ogni creatura. Egli fu fatto il maschio in sé e la femmina in sé, i primi nella mente divina, prima e al di sopra di tutti gli altri.

Pages 1967–1980 of the *De Harmonia Mundi* span two chapters. Chapter 24 (continuing from the previous chunk) resolves objections to divine providence by defending God's — and specifically Christ's — governance of the cosmos and the Church.
